meta.title: Chao turns 40
meta.blurb: Part reunion, part food coma, part shameless excuse to fly Business. 18 friends, 6 countries, one birthday that's going to be very hard to top.
meta.invite:
Hey, it's finally happening. I'm turning 40.

This trip isn't just about celebrating my birthday. It's about celebrating some of the best bits of my life, especially the incredible humans I've collected along the way. That means you.

You've all been part of my story in different ways, across different chapters, countries, continents, adventures, and mildly questionable decisions. So I thought: what better way to mark 40 than by bringing some of my favourite people together for one big trip across Hong Kong, Shenzhen, and Taiwan?

We'll explore new places, eat unknown foods, get a bit lost, laugh a lot, and create the kind of memories we'll still be talking about years from now.

Here's to 40, friendship, food, adventure, and just the right amount of chaos.
meta.playful: 18 friends · 3 cities · 1 very full camera roll

destinations.name: Hong Kong
destinations.blurb: Where the whole crew lands and the birthday kicks off.
destinations.why: If you had to pick one city that is basically Chao in physical form, it's Hong Kong. East meets West, chaos meets order, dim sum at any hour and a skyline that doesn't quit. It's loud, it's alive, and it doesn't apologise for either. So when it came to picking where 18 people from 6 countries would fly halfway across the world to watch one man get old, this was always the answer. No debate needed.

destinations.name: Shenzhen
destinations.blurb: A quick high-speed-rail hop north for a night.
destinations.why: Honestly we just want to go and see some cool stuff. Robots walking the streets, drones in the sky, lights everywhere. Shenzhen is the city of 2050 and we're going to experience it firsthand. Someone might even bring a robot home.

destinations.name: Taipei
destinations.blurb: Night markets, Jiufen, and Taipei Pride to finish.
destinations.why: Asia's best-kept secret and somehow still underrated. A melting pot where Japanese precision, Chinese soul, and Southeast Asian warmth all collide in the best possible way. Closest to where Chao was born, built around food you'll be dreaming about for weeks, and the kind of chill you simply can't manufacture.

days.title: Jiufen: the Ghibli town
days.summary: Lantern alleys, tea houses, golden hour.

plans.plan: ☀️ Early start → Jiufen Old Street. Walk to Shandao Temple MRT → Taipei Main Station → train (TRA) to Ruifang, then bus/taxi up. Arrive before the crowds; tea + breakfast snacks.
🏮 Explore Jiufen: A-Mei Tea House viewpoint, lantern alleys, shops. Street-food crawl: taro balls, peanut ice-cream roll, fish balls, herbal tea. Optional: Jinguashi Gold Museum / Golden Waterfall.
🌇 Stay for golden hour + lanterns, then bus/taxi → Ruifang → train back. Dinner near Dongmen / Yongkang St or Zhongxiao Fuxing.

notes: This is a living list: will be constantly updated.
⚠️ Book Hong Kong stay for the night of 26 Oct.
🇨🇳 Shenzhen is mainland China — sort your China visa or Shenzhen permit and set up Alipay/WeChat Pay before you fly (see the China tips below).

chinaTips.title: Shenzhen survival kit
chinaTips.intro: For most of us this is a first trip to mainland China. Hong Kong and Taipei run on the open internet you already know — but our night in Shenzhen (Sun 25 Oct) is mainland China, where Google, WhatsApp and your usual apps are blocked and almost nobody takes cash or foreign cards in person. Sort these before you fly.

groups.title: Set up mobile payments before you fly
groups.note: Shenzhen is effectively cashless — street stalls, taxis and shops all expect a QR scan, and foreign cards are rarely accepted in person.

items.text: Install BOTH Alipay and WeChat Pay and link a foreign Visa or Mastercard. Set up two, because when one fails at a till the other usually works.
Do it at home on reliable wifi: register with your phone number, complete passport real-name verification (name + face scan), then add your card.
Fees: payments of ¥200 or less are free; a 3% fee applies above that (WeChat waives it for the first 60 days on a new card).

groups.title: Get online past the Great Firewall
groups.note: This only matters in Shenzhen — Hong Kong and Taipei are wide open.

items.text: Blocked in mainland China: Google (Search, Maps, Gmail), YouTube, WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, Messenger and X — all dead without a workaround.

Easiest fix in 2026 is a roaming travel eSIM (Airalo, Holafly, Saily). It routes your data out via Hong Kong or Singapore, so WhatsApp, Google and Instagram just work with no VPN. Buy and install it before you land.

links.label: Why an eSIM beats a VPN in China

items.text: VPNs are unreliable in 2026 after a crackdown, and you can't download one once you're inside China. If you want one as a backup, install it at home first.
Google Maps is blocked and inaccurate on the mainland. iPhone users: just use Apple Maps — it runs on Amap's data, is fully in English and needs no VPN.
Android users (or anyone wanting local detail plus ride-hailing) should download Amap / AMap Global, the English version, before the trip.
DiDi is China's Uber. Use the domestic 'DiDi Chuxing' app — English interface, register with your normal number, pay via Alipay/WeChat, and the chat auto-translates.
At the station or airport, head to the 网约车 (ride-hailing) pickup zone rather than the taxi curb.
The high-speed train runs from Hong Kong West Kowloon to Shenzhen Futian in about 15 minutes, with immigration cleared inside the station.

groups.title: Visa & border — sort this early
groups.note: The big one. Because we cross from Hong Kong and come straight back, the 240-hour visa-free transit does NOT apply. Check your own passport's rules and arrange this before the trip.

items.text: Option A: a proper China tourist (L) visa — the safe choice, especially if any mainland plans change.

Option B: the 5-day Shenzhen Special Economic Zone permit, issued at the Futian / Lok Ma Chau checkpoint (about ¥168, pay by Alipay/WeChat). Valid inside Shenzhen only, open to UK, US, Australia and ~50 other nationalities.
Carry your passport on the mainland — you'll need it for the train, hotel check-in and payment verification.

Download a translation app with the offline Chinese pack before you go (Google Translate — grab the offline pack first — or Baidu Translate, which works locally). The camera menu-translate is a lifesaver.
Keep a little cash (CNY) as backup, but expect to pay for almost everything by QR.
